analogy: Think of "Mineral Nutrition" through the chapter's own opening images: RESPIRATION IN PLANTS 153 All of us breathe to live, but why is breathing so essential to life? Also, do all living organisms, including plants and microbes, breathe? All living organisms need energy for carrying out daily life activities, be it absorption, transport, movement, reproduction... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: RESPIRATION IN PLANTS 153, RESPIRATION IN PLANTS, CHAPTER 12, 12.1 Do Plants. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Mineral Nutrition is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that rESPIRATION IN PLANTS 153 All of us breathe to live, but why is breathing so essential to life?

The text explains that also, do all living organisms, including plants and microbes, breathe?

The text explains that all living organisms need energy for carrying out daily life activities, be it absorption, transport, movement, reproduction or even breathing.

The text explains that we know we eat food for energy – but how is this energy taken from food?

The text explains that and micro-organisms – for their energy requirements, do they eat ‘food’?

The text explains that you may wonder at the several questions raised above – they may seem to be very disconnected.

The text explains that but in reality, the process of breathing is very much connected to the process of release of energy from food.

The text explains that all the energy required for ‘life’ processes is obtained by oxidation of some macromolecules that we call ‘food’.

The text explains that green plants and cyanobacteria can prepare their own food; by the process of photosynthesis they trap light energy and convert it into chemical energy that is stored in the bonds of carbohydrates like glucose, sucrose and starch.

The text explains that we must remember that in green plants too, not all cells, tissues and organs photosynthesise; only cells containing chloroplasts, that are most often located in the superficial layers, carry out photosynthesis.

The text explains that hence, even in green plants all other organs, tissues and cells that are non-green, need food for oxidation.

The text explains that hence, food has to be translocated to all non-green parts.

The text explains that animals are heterotrophic, i.e., they obtain food from plants RESPIRATION IN PLANTS CHAPTER 12 12.1 Do Plants Breathe?

The text explains that 12.2 Glycolysis 12.3 Fermentation 12.4 Aerobic Respiration 12.5 The Respiratory Balance Sheet 12.6 Amphibolic Pathway 12.7 Respiratory Quotient

The text explains that 154 BIOLOGY directly (herbivores) or indirectly (carnivores).

The text explains that saprophytes like fungi are dependent on dead and decaying matter.

The text explains that what is important to recognise is that ultimately all the food that is respired for life processes comes from photosynthesis.

The text explains that this chapter deals with cellular respiration or the mechanism of breakdown of food materials within the cell to release energy, and the trapping of this energy for synthesis of ATP.

The text explains that the compounds that are oxidised during this process are known as respiratory substrates.

The text explains that hence, it is important to understand that the energy released by oxidation in respiration is not (or rather cannot be) used directly but is used to synthesise ATP, which is broken down whenever (and wherever) energy needs to be utilised.

The text explains that this energy trapped in ATP is utilised in various energy-requiring processes of the organisms, and the carbon skeleton produced during respiration is used as precursors for biosynthesis of other molecules in the cell.

The text explains that well, the answer to this question is not quite so direct.

The text explains that hence, plants have systems in place that ensure the availability of O2.

The text explains that there are several reasons why plants can get along without respiratory organs.

The text explains that the cells in the interior are dead and provide only mechanical support.

The text explains that during the process of respiration, oxygen is utilised, and carbon dioxide, water and energy are released as products.

The text explains that there are sufficient reasons to believe that the first cells on this planet lived in an atmosphere that lacked oxygen.

The text explains that in anaerobic organisms, it is the only process in respiration.

The text explains that in this process, glucose undergoes partial oxidation to form two molecules of pyruvic acid.

The text explains that the conversion of BPGA to 3-phosphoglyceric acid (PGA), is also an energy yielding process; this energy is trapped by the formation of ATP.

The text explains that can you then calculate how many ATP Can you then calculate how many ATP Can you then calculate how many ATP Can you then calculate how many ATP Can you then calculate how many ATP molecules are directly synthesised in this molecules are directly synthesised in this molecules are directly synthesised in this molecules are directly synthesised in this molecules are directly synthesised in this pathway from one glucose molecule? pathway from one glucose molecule? pathway from one glucose molecule? pathway from one glucose molecule? pathway from one glucose molecule?

The text explains that the reducing agent is NADH+H+ which is reoxidised to NAD+ in both the processes.

The text explains that also, the processes are hazardous – either acid or alcohol is produced.

The text explains that what then is the process by which organisms can carry out complete oxidation of glucose and extract the energy stored to Figure 12.2 Major pathways of anaerobic respiration

The text explains that aerobic respiration is the process that leads to a complete oxidation of organic substances in the presence of oxygen, and releases CO2, water and a large amount of energy present in the substrate.

The text explains that we will look at these processes in the next section.

The text explains that what is interesting to note is that the first process takes place in the matrix of the mitochondria while the second process is located on the inner membrane of the mitochondria.

The text explains that pyruvic acid CoA NAD Mg Pyruvate dehydrogenase + +  → + + 2   + + + + Acetyl CoA CO NADH H 2 During this process, two molecules of NADH are produced from the metabolism of two molecules of pyruvic acid (produced from one glucose molecule during glycolysis).

The text explains that 12.4.2 Electron Transport System (ETS) and Oxidative Phosphorylation The following steps in the respiratory process are to release and utilise the energy stored in NADH+H+ and FADH2.

The text explains that this is accomplished when they are oxidised through the electron transport system and the electrons are passed on to O2 resulting in the formation of H2O.

The text explains that the metabolic pathway through which the electron passes from one carrier to another, is called the electron transport system (ETS) (Figure 12.4) and it is present in the inner mitochondrial membrane.

The text explains that although the aerobic process of respiration takes place only in the presence of oxygen, the role of oxygen is limited to the terminal stage of the process.

The text explains that yet, the presence of oxygen is vital, since it drives the whole process by removing hydrogen from the system.

The text explains that unlike photophosphorylation where it is the light energy that is utilised for the production of proton gradient required for phosphorylation, in respiration it is the energy of oxidation-reduction utilised for the same process.

The text explains that it is for this reason that the process is called oxidative phosphorylation.

The text explains that you have already studied about the mechanism of membrane-linked ATP synthesis as explained by chemiosmotic hypothesis in the earlier chapter.

The text explains that the respiratory pathway is an amphibolic pathway as it involves both anabolism and catabolism.

The text explains that the respiratory quotient depends upon the type of respiratory substance used during respiration.

The text explains that differentiate between (a) Respiration and Combustion (b) Glycolysis and Krebs’ cycle (c) Aerobic respiration and Fermentation 2.

The text explains that give the schematic representation of glycolysis?

The text explains that what are the main steps in aerobic respiration?

The text explains that give the schematic representation of an overall view of Krebs’ cycle.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12: Mineral Nutrition

prelims_traps: Do not treat RESPIRATION IN PLANTS 153 and RESPIRATION IN PLANTS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Mineral Nutrition; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Mineral Nutrition through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use RESPIRATION IN PLANTS 153 and RESPIRATION IN PLANTS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
